name: Rhenium
name_origin: Named after the Rhine River (Latin 'Rhenus') in honor of Walter Noddack's homeland, Germany, by its discoverers in 1925.

discovery.discoverer: Walter Noddack, Ida Tacke, and Otto Berg

occurrence.abundance_summary: Rhenium is one of the rarest elements in Earth's crust, at about 0.7 ppb. It has no minerals where it is the major constituent and is recovered almost entirely as a byproduct of molybdenum refining, which itself is a byproduct of porphyry copper mining. Chile, the United States, and Poland are the leading producers.
states: trace in molybdenite (MoS2) at up to 0.2%
trace in flue dusts of Mo roasters
rare native mineral rheniite (ReS2) in volcanic fumaroles

compounds.name: Rhenium heptoxide
compounds.summary: A pale yellow volatile oxide that is the most stable oxide of rhenium and a precursor to perrhenate catalysts.

compounds.name: Perrhenic acid
compounds.summary: A strong acid whose perrhenate salts are used as superalloy precursors and in olefin metathesis catalysis.

compounds.name: Rhenium disulfide
compounds.summary: A layered semiconductor with strongly anisotropic optical and electronic properties, of interest for 2D electronics.

compounds.name: Rhenium hexafluoride
compounds.summary: A yellow crystalline solid that melts near room temperature, used in chemical vapor deposition.

compounds.name: Dirhenium decacarbonyl
compounds.summary: A colorless metal-carbonyl compound used as a starting point for rhenium organometallic chemistry.

uses: Single-crystal nickel-based superalloys for high-pressure turbine blades in jet engines
Platinum-rhenium reforming catalysts for production of high-octane gasoline
Thermocouples (W-Re alloys) capable of operating above 2000 C
X-ray tube targets and electrical contacts where wear and high temperatures are issues
Filaments in mass spectrometers and ion gauges
Olefin metathesis catalysts using methylrhenium trioxide (MTO)
reactivity_summary: Rhenium is a dense silvery-white metal with one of the highest melting points (3459 K) and one of the broadest ranges of oxidation states (-3 to +7). Bulk rhenium does not react with water or air at room temperature but slowly tarnishes in moist air; the metal oxidizes rapidly above 400 C to volatile Re2O7. It dissolves in oxidizing acids (HNO3, concentrated H2SO4) but is essentially inert to non-oxidizing acids and resists liquid metal attack remarkably well.
isotopes_summary: Rhenium has two naturally occurring isotopes: 185Re (~37.4%, stable) and 187Re (~62.6%, beta-radioactive with t1/2 ~4.1x10^10 yr). The slow 187Re->187Os decay forms the basis of the Re-Os geochronometer used to date sulfide ores and meteorites. The medical isotope 188Re, produced from a 188W generator, is used in targeted radiotherapy for bone metastases.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Rhenium
